label: Palo Alto Health Baseline
command:
show system info
show system disk-space files
show system logdb-quota
request license info
show jobs processed
description:
This runbook establishes a fast, read-only health baseline for a Palo Alto Networks firewall or Panorama-managed appliance using native PAN-OS CLI commands such as #show system info, #show system disk-space files, #show system logdb-quota, #request license info, and #show jobs processed. Operators use it at the start of incident response, pre-change validation, or post-upgrade verification to understand the device identity, software context, storage pressure, log database capacity, license posture, and recent background job activity without modifying the running configuration.

  1. How it works: the operator queries PAN-OS system metadata to confirm the platform model, serial, software version, and management state exposed by the management plane.
  2. How it works: storage and log-database commands are used to spot early indicators of operational trouble such as partition exhaustion, inode pressure, or logging constraints that can silently degrade visibility.
  3. How it works: licensing and job-history checks reveal whether content updates, downloads, commits, or upgrades recently succeeded, failed, or are still relevant to the current troubleshooting timeline.
  4. How it works: the combined output becomes a baseline snapshot that informs whether the next step should be routing analysis, session triage, packet capture, or a controlled maintenance action.
  • Tool Location/Source: all commands are built into the PAN-OS CLI and are available from the local console, SSH management access, or Panorama administrative CLI sessions. Primary source: docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/cli-cheat-sheet-device-management.md:19-72.
  • Attack pre-requesite: valid CLI access to the firewall or Panorama appliance with permission to run operational show and request commands on the management plane.
  • What attacker gain: device model and version details, disk and log database health, installed license visibility, and job-history context that can expose operational weakness or recent administrative activity.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: restrict management-plane reachability, enforce MFA and role-based admin access, log all CLI sessions, and review unusual baseline collection from unapproved source IPs or emergency accounts because this workflow is low-noise and attractive for post-access reconnaissance.

Core Commands:

> show system info
> show system disk-space files
> show system logdb-quota
> request license info
> show jobs processed

label: PAN-OS Performance Snapshot
command:
show system software status
show system resources
show running resource-monitor
show system statistics
description:
This runbook captures a focused performance baseline for the Palo Alto Networks management plane and dataplane using #show system software status, #show system resources, #show running resource-monitor, and #show system statistics. It is the fastest source-backed workflow in the approved corpus for validating whether a slowdown is rooted in management-plane process issues, dataplane resource pressure, or elevated packet and throughput load.

  1. How it works: the operator checks running software state to determine whether management-plane processes are healthy enough to support normal control operations.
  2. How it works: management-plane resource output reveals CPU and memory pressure that can affect commits, log processing, and administrator responsiveness.
  3. How it works: dataplane resource-monitor output highlights packet-processing stress that may affect sessions, inspection, and forwarding behavior.
  4. How it works: system statistics are correlated with the resource views to decide whether the next step should be session triage, packet capture, routing analysis, or a maintenance window.
  • Tool Location/Source: native PAN-OS operational CLI commands. Primary references: docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/cli-cheat-sheet-device-management.md:40-59 and docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/kb-monitor-live-sessions-cli.md:63-73.
  • Attack pre-requesite: authenticated CLI access to the firewall or Panorama appliance with permission to query management-plane and dataplane operational status.
  • What attacker gain: visibility into process health, current load, throughput, packet rate, and whether the appliance is under operational strain that may reduce defensive effectiveness.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: alert on unexpected management-plane access, lock down admin roles, and preserve CLI auditing because this baseline is a low-noise way to assess firewall health after initial access.

Core Commands:

> show system software status
> show system resources
> show running resource-monitor
> show system statistics

label: Live Session Triage
command:
show session info
show system statistics
show session all
description:
This runbook inspects real-time Palo Alto session pressure using #show session info, #show system statistics, and #show session all. It is a high-signal operational workflow for understanding how many sessions are active, how heavily the session table is utilized, whether accelerated aging is engaged, and what representative flows are currently consuming dataplane state.

  1. How it works: the operator queries the session subsystem to measure active sessions, protocol distribution, table utilization, and current packet and throughput rates.
  2. How it works: the output also reveals timeout behavior, accelerated aging thresholds, and whether hardware session offloading is active.
  3. How it works: a full live session listing provides a sample of actual flows, zones, ports, and application states for immediate troubleshooting context.
  4. How it works: the combined view determines whether the device is simply busy, nearing session pressure, or carrying obviously abnormal flows.
  • Tool Location/Source: native PAN-OS session and statistics commands. Primary references: docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/cli-cheat-sheet-device-management.md:75-87 and docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/kb-monitor-live-sessions-cli.md:20-88.
  • Attack pre-requesite: authenticated CLI access with permission to query live session and traffic statistics.
  • What attacker gain: current flow volume, protocol mix, throughput, timeout posture, offload state, and examples of active network conversations across zones.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: because session inspection is read-only and quiet, management-plane auditing and strict admin-source controls are the main defenses against misuse.

Core Commands:

> show session info
> show system statistics
> show session all

label: Session Hunt and Pivot
command:
show session all filter
show session all filter source <source-ip>
show session id <session-id>
description:
This runbook pivots from broad Palo Alto session visibility into targeted flow hunting with #show session all filter, #show session all filter source, and #show session id. It is used when an operator already knows a suspicious source, rule, NAT behavior, or session identifier and needs to isolate the exact flow record rather than scanning the entire live session table.

  1. How it works: the operator queries the supported filter keys to learn which session attributes can be used for narrowing the search.
  2. How it works: flow records are then filtered by source, destination, rule, NAT rule, user, protocol, state, or other supported selectors.
  3. How it works: once a specific session is identified, the operator pivots to the session ID for a focused per-flow inspection.
  4. How it works: this reduces noise and accelerates troubleshooting for a single conversation, host, or policy decision.
  • Tool Location/Source: native PAN-OS session-filtering commands. Primary references: docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/kb-monitor-live-sessions-cli.md:90-127 and docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/cli-cheat-sheet-device-management.md:82-87.
  • Attack pre-requesite: authenticated CLI access and at least one known attribute such as source IP, destination IP, rule name, NAT rule, or session ID.
  • What attacker gain: precise visibility into one flow’s state, translation behavior, user context, and policy alignment.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: session hunting is operationally normal, so the main defensive controls are management access restriction, session logging, and alerting on suspicious CLI access from unusual admin paths.

Core Commands:

> show session all filter
> show session all filter source <source-ip>
> show session id <session-id>

label: Policy Match Validation
command:
show running security-policy
test security-policy-match application <application> source-user <domain\user> destination <dst-ip> destination-port <dst-port> source <src-ip> protocol <protocol-number>
test authentication-policy-match from <from-zone> to <to-zone> source <src-ip> destination <dst-ip>
test decryption-policy-match category <url-category> from <from-zone> source <src-ip> destination <dst-ip>
description:
This runbook validates Palo Alto policy decisions before or after a change by using #show running security-policy, #test security-policy-match, #test authentication-policy-match, and #test decryption-policy-match. It allows operators to model how traffic should be handled by security, authentication, and decryption policies without generating production user traffic or waiting for logs to accumulate.

  1. How it works: the operator reviews the running policy context to understand what rulebase is active on the firewall.
  2. How it works: a synthetic security-policy test predicts the rule that would match a specific user, application, source, destination, port, and protocol combination.
  3. How it works: authentication-policy and decryption-policy tests provide the same pre-flight validation for identity enforcement and SSL inspection outcomes.
  4. How it works: the returned matched rule becomes a safe decision point before modifying rules or escalating a complaint to another team.
  • Tool Location/Source: native PAN-OS test commands. Primary references: docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/cli-cheat-sheet-device-management.md:89-94 and docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/test-policy-matches.md:11-46.
  • Attack pre-requesite: CLI access and enough knowledge of the test flow to supply source, destination, protocol, application, user, or URL category inputs.
  • What attacker gain: confidence about which rule would apply to a flow, whether authentication would be required, and whether decryption would occur.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: these are read-only control-plane simulations, so they are low noise; enforce admin auditing and least-privilege CLI roles to prevent unauthorized policy reconnaissance.

Core Commands:

> show running security-policy
> test security-policy-match application <application> source-user <domain\user> destination <dst-ip> destination-port <dst-port> source <src-ip> protocol <protocol-number>
> test authentication-policy-match from <from-zone> to <to-zone> source <src-ip> destination <dst-ip>
> test decryption-policy-match category <url-category> from <from-zone> source <src-ip> destination <dst-ip>

label: Rule-Based PCAP Workflow
command:
set application dump on rule <security-rule-name>
set application dump-unknown no
configure
set deviceconfig setting application dump-unknown off
commit
set application dump off
scp export filter-pcap from <capture> to <username@host:path>
tftp export filter-pcap from <capture> to <tftp-host>
description:
This runbook enables packet capture tied to a specific Palo Alto security rule using #set application dump on rule, controls whether unknown traffic is captured with #set application dump-unknown, and exports the resulting PCAPs with #scp export filter-pcap or #tftp export filter-pcap. It is the most precise source-backed workflow in the approved corpus for collecting troubleshooting evidence when the operator already knows which security rule should be seeing the traffic.

  1. How it works: the operator enables application dump on a named rule so packets tied to that policy path are captured for inspection.
  2. How it works: unknown or insufficient-data capture can be disabled temporarily or persistently to reduce irrelevant packet collection noise.
  3. How it works: after the traffic is captured, the PCAP files are exported directly from the CLI to a remote host or Palo Alto support case.
  4. How it works: the workflow must be closed cleanly because Palo Alto explicitly warns that packet capture is CPU-intensive and can degrade firewall performance.
  • Tool Location/Source: native PAN-OS CLI plus CLI export functions. Primary references: docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/kb-pcap-specific-rule.md:23-117, docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/kb-export-pcap-cli.md:22-47, and docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/take-packet-captures.md:11-15.
  • Attack pre-requesite: CLI access, a known security rule name, sufficient privilege to modify operational capture behavior, and network reachability to an SCP or TFTP destination if exporting externally.
  • What attacker gain: rule-scoped packet evidence, visibility into matching flows, and portable PCAP artifacts for offline analysis or vendor escalation.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: packet capture can stress the firewall and should be tightly controlled, monitored, and disabled promptly; defenders should alert on unexpected capture enablement and restrict capture privileges to trusted admins only.

Core Commands:

> set application dump on rule <security-rule-name>
> set application dump-unknown no
> configure
# set deviceconfig setting application dump-unknown off
# commit
> set application dump off
scp export filter-pcap from <capture> to <username@host:path>
tftp export filter-pcap from <capture> to <tftp-host>

label: Commit Control Workflow
command:
configure
validate full
show jobs all
show jobs id <job-id>
commit
commit partial admin <admin> vsys <vsys-name> device-and-network excluded
commit partial device-and-network excluded
description:
This runbook controls Palo Alto configuration activation by combining #validate full, #show jobs all, #show jobs id, #commit, and #commit partial. It is a production-safe workflow for confirming that the candidate configuration is valid, understanding whether the commit queue is already busy, and limiting blast radius with targeted partial commits.

  1. How it works: candidate changes are validated before activation so syntax and semantic problems are discovered before affecting the running configuration.
  2. How it works: the operator checks commit job records to confirm whether validation or commit actions succeeded, failed, or are still queued.
  3. How it works: a full commit can activate the entire candidate configuration, while partial commits reduce scope to a specific admin, vsys, or excluded configuration area.
  4. How it works: this workflow prevents accidental broad deployment and gives the operator a controlled path through strict change windows.
  • Tool Location/Source: native PAN-OS configuration-mode and operational CLI commands. Primary source: docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/commit-configuration-changes.md:14-101.
  • Attack pre-requesite: configuration privileges on the firewall or Panorama node and an existing candidate configuration change to validate or commit.
  • What attacker gain: confirmation that pending changes are valid, insight into prior commit behavior, and the ability to activate only selected portions of a change set.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: log all commit attempts, enforce change control, limit commit rights by role, and review unexpected partial commits because they can be used to activate isolated malicious changes quietly.

Core Commands:

> configure
# validate full
> show jobs all
> show jobs id <job-id>
> configure
# commit
# commit partial admin <admin> vsys <vsys-name> device-and-network excluded
# commit partial device-and-network excluded

label: Basic Routing Validation
command:
show routing route
show routing fib virtual-router <name> | match <x.x.x.x/Y>
set system setting arp-cache-timeout <60-65536>
show system setting arp-cache-timeout
ping host <destination-ip-address>
ping source <ip-address-on-dataplane> host <destination-ip-address>
show netstat statistics yes
description:
This runbook verifies basic Palo Alto forwarding behavior with #show routing route, #show routing fib virtual-router, #show system setting arp-cache-timeout, #ping host, #ping source, and #show netstat statistics yes. It is the first routing workflow to run before moving into advanced logical-router, OSPF, or BGP analysis.

  1. How it works: the operator checks the routing table and forwarding information for a destination to confirm that the firewall knows how it intends to route the traffic.
  2. How it works: ARP cache timeout settings are reviewed or adjusted when neighboring resolution behavior is part of the problem.
  3. How it works: reachability is tested from both the management interface and a dataplane source address to distinguish control-path versus forwarding-path problems.
  4. How it works: network statistics provide a lightweight final check for unusual socket-level behavior or interface-side anomalies.
  • Tool Location/Source: native PAN-OS networking CLI commands. Primary source: docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/cli-cheat-sheet-networking.md:14-40 and docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/cli-cheat-sheet-networking.md:212-231.
  • Attack pre-requesite: CLI access plus knowledge of the destination network, relevant virtual router, and a usable dataplane source address when testing from the forwarding path.
  • What attacker gain: visibility into route selection, forwarding intent, ARP behavior, and whether traffic fails from management only or from the dataplane as well.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: route and connectivity checks are normal admin actions, so defenders should rely on management-plane controls and CLI auditing rather than expecting these commands to be noisy.

Core Commands:

> show routing route
> show routing fib virtual-router <name> | match <x.x.x.x/Y>
> set system setting arp-cache-timeout <60-65536>
> show system setting arp-cache-timeout
> ping host <destination-ip-address>
> ping source <ip-address-on-dataplane> host <destination-ip-address>
> show netstat statistics yes

label: Advanced Routing Inspection
command:
show advanced-routing fib
show advanced-routing route destination <ip/netmask>
show advanced-routing logical-router <logical-router-name>
show advanced-routing resource logical-router <logical-router-name>
show advanced-routing static-route-path-monitor
show advanced-routing ospf neighbor
show advanced-routing ospfv3 neighbor
show advanced-routing bgp summary logical-router <logical-router-name>
show advanced-routing bgp peer detail peer-name <peer-name> logical-router <logical-router-name>
description:
This runbook escalates Palo Alto routing analysis into the advanced-routing subsystem with #show advanced-routing fib, #show advanced-routing route, #show advanced-routing logical-router, #show advanced-routing ospf, #show advanced-routing ospfv3, and #show advanced-routing bgp. It is the source-backed workflow for investigating logical-router state, FIB/RIB divergence, static-route path monitoring, dynamic neighbor health, and BGP route visibility.

  1. How it works: the operator inspects advanced FIB and RIB views to verify how the firewall currently intends to forward traffic and which route source is active.
  2. How it works: logical-router and resource commands expose routing-domain state and the resources allocated to it.
  3. How it works: OSPF and OSPFv3 commands reveal neighbor, LSDB, summary, and graceful-restart state when dynamic adjacency issues are suspected.
  4. How it works: BGP summary, peer, and route commands expose the received, filtered, dampened, and advertised route sets used to diagnose control-plane path issues.
  • Tool Location/Source: native PAN-OS advanced-routing CLI. Primary source: docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/cli-cheat-sheet-networking.md:234-427.
  • Attack pre-requesite: CLI access plus knowledge of the logical router, peer name, route family, or protocol domain being investigated.
  • What attacker gain: deep visibility into route origin, neighbor health, policy filtering, and dynamic routing behavior across the firewall.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: advanced routing inspection is still read-only, so the security boundary is management-plane access control, audit retention, and strong admin-role separation.

Core Commands:

> show advanced-routing fib
> show advanced-routing route destination <ip/netmask>
> show advanced-routing logical-router <logical-router-name>
> show advanced-routing resource logical-router <logical-router-name>
> show advanced-routing static-route-path-monitor
> show advanced-routing ospf neighbor
> show advanced-routing ospfv3 neighbor
> show advanced-routing bgp summary logical-router <logical-router-name>
> show advanced-routing bgp peer detail peer-name <peer-name> logical-router <logical-router-name>

label: NAT Policy Inspection
command:
show running nat-policy
test nat-policy-match
show running ippool
show running global-ippool
description:
This runbook validates Palo Alto translation logic using #show running nat-policy, #test nat-policy-match, #show running ippool, and #show running global-ippool. It is the right workflow when the route is present but traffic still behaves incorrectly because the translation policy or address-pool state is wrong.

  1. How it works: the operator reviews the active NAT policy to understand which translations are configured on the firewall.
  2. How it works: a NAT match test predicts which policy should translate a flow, similar to a dry-run for the translation engine.
  3. How it works: local and global IP pool views reveal whether translation resources exist and are being consumed as expected.
  4. How it works: the combined result separates routing problems from translation problems before deeper packet inspection begins.
  • Tool Location/Source: native PAN-OS NAT-related CLI. Primary source: docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/cli-cheat-sheet-networking.md:51-72.
  • Attack pre-requesite: CLI access and enough flow context to run a NAT match test meaningfully.
  • What attacker gain: translation policy visibility, predicted NAT outcome, and address-pool availability information.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: translation inspection is quiet and administrative, so protecting the management plane and preserving full CLI auditing are the key safeguards.

Core Commands:

> show running nat-policy
> test nat-policy-match
> show running ippool
> show running global-ippool

label: IPSec Health Check
command:
show vpn flow
show vpn gateway
show vpn ike-sa
show vpn ipsec-sa
show vpn tunnel
set network tunnel ipsec <name_of_tunnel> ipsec-mode [tunnel | transport]
description:
This runbook inspects Palo Alto IPSec state with #show vpn flow, #show vpn gateway, #show vpn ike-sa, #show vpn ipsec-sa, and #show vpn tunnel. It is the source-backed operational workflow for determining whether a tunnel exists, whether IKE phase 1 and phase 2 are healthy, and whether the configured tunnel mode aligns with the intended deployment.

  1. How it works: the operator checks VPN counters and gateway definitions to establish the configured IPSec context on the firewall.
  2. How it works: IKE and IPSec security associations are inspected separately so failures in phase 1 and phase 2 can be distinguished cleanly.
  3. How it works: the tunnel listing provides confirmation of the auto-key IPSec tunnel objects participating in the exchange.
  4. How it works: if needed, the operator can verify or set the tunnel mode to ensure the policy intent matches the deployed configuration.
  • Tool Location/Source: native PAN-OS VPN CLI. Primary source: docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/cli-cheat-sheet-networking.md:74-115.
  • Attack pre-requesite: CLI access and knowledge of the relevant tunnel or gateway name when making targeted configuration checks.
  • What attacker gain: tunnel inventory, phase 1 and phase 2 state, VPN counters, and confirmation of tunnel mode.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: VPN state inspection is low noise; secure management access and audit operator activity because these commands reveal valuable network perimeter information.

Core Commands:

> show vpn flow
> show vpn gateway
> show vpn ike-sa
> show vpn ipsec-sa
> show vpn tunnel
> set network tunnel ipsec <name_of_tunnel> ipsec-mode [tunnel | transport]

label: GlobalProtect Satellite Check
command:
request global-protect-portal set-satellite-cookie-expiration value <0-5>
show global-protect-portal satellite-cookie-expiration
show global-protect-satellite satellite
description:
This runbook validates the limited GlobalProtect / LSVPN visibility present in the approved Palo Alto source set using #request global-protect-portal set-satellite-cookie-expiration, #show global-protect-portal satellite-cookie-expiration, and #show global-protect-satellite satellite. It is not a full SSL VPN gateway troubleshooting workflow; instead, it focuses on satellite cookie lifetime and the current satellite authentication-cookie state.

  1. How it works: the operator checks or modifies the portal-side satellite cookie expiration value.
  2. How it works: the current cookie lifetime is confirmed to ensure the intended portal setting is active.
  3. How it works: the satellite command displays the current satellite authentication-cookie generation state on the LSVPN side.
  4. How it works: together these commands provide a narrow but valid GlobalProtect-adjacent health check from the current source corpus.
  • Tool Location/Source: native PAN-OS LSVPN / GlobalProtect-related CLI commands. Primary source: docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/cli-cheat-sheet-networking.md:117-137.
  • Attack pre-requesite: CLI access and administrative responsibility for the relevant GlobalProtect portal or satellite deployment.
  • What attacker gain: portal cookie-lifetime settings and limited visibility into satellite authentication-cookie generation state.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: because this source set only exposes limited operational commands, defenders should still treat any unexpected management-plane inspection of remote-access infrastructure as sensitive activity.

Core Commands:

> request global-protect-portal set-satellite-cookie-expiration value <0-5>
> show global-protect-portal satellite-cookie-expiration
> show global-protect-satellite satellite

label: User-ID Mapping Triage
command:
show user user-id-agent state all
show user server-monitor state all
show user server-monitor statistics
show user group-mapping statistics
show user group-mapping state all
show user ip-user-mapping all
show user ip-user-mapping ip <ip-address>
show log userid datasourcetype equal kerberos
show log userid datasourcetype equal globalprotect
show log userid datasource equal vpn-client
less mp-log authd.log
clear user-cache all
clear user-cache ip <ip-address/netmask>
description:
This runbook inspects Palo Alto identity mapping health using #show user user-id-agent state all, #show user server-monitor state all, #show user group-mapping statistics, #show user ip-user-mapping, #show log userid, and #less mp-log authd.log. It is the strongest source-backed identity troubleshooting workflow in the approved corpus for proving whether policy decisions that depend on users and groups are being fed by healthy mapping sources.

  1. How it works: the operator verifies whether User-ID agents and integrated server-monitor functions are configured and ingesting events.
  2. How it works: group-mapping and IP-to-user mapping views confirm whether users and groups are actually being resolved on the firewall.
  3. How it works: userid log queries attribute mappings to specific data sources such as Kerberos, GlobalProtect, or vpn-client style sources defined by the documented syntax.
  4. How it works: the authentication daemon log and cache-clear options help determine whether the issue is stale state, missing ingestion, or wrong identity source selection.
  • Tool Location/Source: native PAN-OS User-ID and authd CLI. Primary references: docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/cli-cheat-sheet-user-id.md:15-140 and docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/cli-cheat-sheet-device-management.md:96-100.
  • Attack pre-requesite: CLI access and a user, group, IP, or data source worth investigating.
  • What attacker gain: visibility into the identity sources feeding policy, current IP-user mappings, group mapping state, and authentication daemon behavior.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: identity mapping is sensitive because it exposes who the firewall believes users are; restrict CLI access and monitor unusual queries against User-ID and authd data.

Core Commands:

> show user user-id-agent state all
> show user server-monitor state all
> show user server-monitor statistics
> show user group-mapping statistics
> show user group-mapping state all
> show user ip-user-mapping all
> show user ip-user-mapping ip <ip-address>
> show log userid datasourcetype equal kerberos
> show log userid datasourcetype equal globalprotect
> show log userid datasource equal vpn-client
> less mp-log authd.log
clear user-cache all
clear user-cache ip <ip-address/netmask>

label: Content-ID Brotli Validation
command:
show system setting brotli
set system setting brotli-encoding enable
show system setting brotli-encoding
set system setting brotli-encoding disable
description:
This runbook validates the narrow Content-ID workflow supported by the approved Palo Alto source set using #show system setting brotli, #set system setting brotli-encoding enable, and #show system setting brotli-encoding. It is specifically about Brotli-encoded HTTP decompression support and operational status, not a general anti-spyware or URL-filtering administration runbook.

  1. How it works: the operator verifies whether the firewall platform supports Brotli decompression and whether the feature is currently enabled.
  2. How it works: the feature can then be enabled or disabled explicitly through the PAN-OS CLI.
  3. How it works: follow-up verification confirms the operational state and can also expose processing statistics tied to Brotli handling.
  4. How it works: because the source text ties this feature to specific PAN-OS versions and supported platforms, the operator must check platform fit before assuming availability.
  • Tool Location/Source: native PAN-OS Content-ID CLI. Primary source: docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/cli-cheat-sheet-content-id.md:15-108.
  • Attack pre-requesite: CLI access and a firewall platform and PAN-OS version that support Brotli decompression as documented in the source.
  • What attacker gain: confirmation of whether Brotli decompression is supported, enabled, and processing traffic as expected.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: changes to content-processing settings should be controlled by change management and configuration auditing because they affect traffic inspection behavior.

Core Commands:

show system setting brotli
set system setting brotli-encoding enable
show system setting brotli-encoding
set system setting brotli-encoding disable
show system setting brotli

label: HA Cluster Review
command:
show high-availability cluster all
show high-availability cluster flap-statistics
show high-availability cluster ha4-status
show high-availability cluster ha4-backup-status
show high-availability cluster session-synchronization
show high-availability cluster state
show high-availability cluster statistics
clear high-availability cluster statistics
request high-availability cluster clear-cache
request high-availability cluster sync-from
description:
This runbook reviews Palo Alto high-availability state using #show high-availability cluster all, #show high-availability cluster state, #show high-availability cluster session-synchronization, and related statistics and cache commands. It is the primary source-backed workflow for determining whether failover state, HA4 transport, synchronized session behavior, or cluster counters explain service instability.

  1. How it works: the operator checks cluster-wide configuration, state, flap counters, and HA4 transport status to establish the health of the HA relationship.
  2. How it works: session-synchronization output reveals how state is being exchanged between HA nodes.
  3. How it works: cluster statistics expose dropped packets and other transport-side anomalies that may affect high-availability behavior.
  4. How it works: when appropriate, statistics can be cleared, session cache can be reset, and a full synchronization can be requested to validate recovery.
  • Tool Location/Source: native PAN-OS HA CLI. Primary source: docs/sources-runbooks/paloalto/cli-cheat-sheet-ha.md:11-82.
  • Attack pre-requesite: CLI access to an HA-capable firewall cluster and the operational authority to run HA maintenance commands where needed.
  • What attacker gain: visibility into cluster health, flap history, HA4 transport state, synchronization behavior, and corrective maintenance options.
  • Detection/Defense opsec: HA commands are powerful operational controls; restrict them to a small set of trusted admins and monitor for unexpected cache clears or sync requests.

Core Commands:

> show high-availability cluster all
> show high-availability cluster flap-statistics
> show high-availability cluster ha4-status
> show high-availability cluster ha4-backup-status
> show high-availability cluster session-synchronization
> show high-availability cluster state
> show high-availability cluster statistics
> clear high-availability cluster statistics
> request high-availability cluster clear-cache
> request high-availability cluster sync-from
